text:
La Roque-Gageac in the Dordogne valley is built directly against the cliff face with the golden limestone houses pressing up against the rock and the Dordogne river running immediately below.

The combination of the extraordinary position, the Renaissance chateau, and the exotic gardens growing in the microclimate created by the south-facing cliff make it one of the finest villages in France.

The village is freely walkable and the traditional gabarre flat-bottomed boat trips on the Dordogne cost around 10 euros.

Those who arrive by canoe from Carsac and see La Roque-Gageac appear around the bend in the river with the cliff rising behind the village describe it as the finest river village approach available anywhere in the Dordogne valley.
In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is a scenic limestone canyon featuring dramatic sheer cliffs, narrow balcony roads, and whitewater rapids. #travel #France

The Chaine des Puys in the Auvergne is a chain of 80 volcanic cones, craters, and lava domes that erupted between 95,000 and 7,000 years ago and is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

The Puy de Dome, the highest of the chain at 1,465 metres, is accessible by a free walking path and provides extraordinary views across the entire volcanic chain.

Those who hike the Chemin des Muletiers to the Puy de Dome summit in early morning when the lower craters are filled with cloud and the volcanic chain stretches north and south across the horizon describe it as the most dramatic and most completely unexpected volcanic landscape available on any free walk in France.

In France, this hidden gem in Aude is a picturesque hillside village featuring a historic restored windmill, a legendary medieval church, and stunning views of nearby Cathar fortresses. #travel #France

The Roman city of Orange in the Vaucluse contains the finest surviving Roman theatre in the western world, with the original stage wall still standing 37 metres high and 103 metres wide, the only intact example in the Roman world.

The theatre still seats 9,000 people for the annual Chorégies opera festival each summer. Entry to the theatre costs around 13 euros.

Those who attend a performance in the Roman theatre in July, with the extraordinary 2,000-year-old stage wall behind the orchestra and the summer evening darkening above describe it as the most extraordinary open-air performance experience available anywhere in France.

In France, this hidden gem in the Aude is a medieval riverside village featuring a majestic Benedictine abbey, a 14th-century covered market, and a picturesque 12th-century stone bridge. #travel #France

Peyrepertuse in the Aude is a Cathar castle perched on a narrow limestone ridge at 800 metres altitude in the Corbieres massif, with walls following the exact contours of the extraordinary rocky spine.

The combination of the extraordinary position and the extraordinary preservation of the medieval fortifications makes it the most dramatically situated medieval castle in France.

Entry to Peyrepertuse costs around 8 euros.

Those who climb to the highest section of the castle, the Saint-Georges citadel, and look out across the Corbieres landscape to the Mediterranean visible on clear days and the Pyrenees rising to the south describe it as the most physically exhilarating and most visually extraordinary castle experience available anywhere in southern France.
In France, this hidden gem in Aude is a magnificent Cistercian monastery featuring Romanesque cloisters, vibrant terraced gardens, and unique modern stained glass. #travel #France

The GR34 coastal path around Brittany stretches for 2,000 kilometres along one of the most dramatically varied coastlines in Europe, from the granite headlands of the Pink Granite Coast to the extraordinary Crozon Peninsula, the extraordinary Cap Frehel, and the extraordinary estuary landscapes of the Morbihan.

The finest sections are as dramatically beautiful as any coastal path in Europe.

Those who walk the section between Perros-Guirec and Trebeurden on the Pink Granite Coast in summer, where the extraordinary pink and orange granite boulders crowd the shoreline and the heather is in bloom above the sea, consistently describe it as the most visually extraordinary free coastal walk available anywhere in France.
In France, this hidden gem in Aude is a preserved Roman thoroughfare featuring ancient limestone slabs, deep chariot ruts, and historical significance as the first road in Gaul. #travel #France

The Grotte de la Deveze near Olargues in the Herault and the better-known Gouffre de Padirac near Rocamadour in the Lot both offer extraordinary underground river boat trips, but Padirac is the most spectacular, with flat-bottomed boats carrying visitors across an underground lake and then along a river beneath extraordinary stalactite formations in chambers 75 metres below the surface.

Entry to Padirac costs around 15 euros.

Those who take the boat across the underground lake and walk through the extraordinary riverside galleries of stalactites and columns deep below the surface describe it as the most dramatically beautiful and most completely extraordinary cave experience available anywhere in France.

In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is an ancient hilltop settlement featuring pre-Roman ruins, a massive complex of silos, and panoramic views of the unique sun-shaped fields below. #travel #France

Saint-Cirq-Lapopie in the Lot valley perches on a cliff 100 metres above the Lot river with medieval houses in extraordinary condition lining the narrow lanes from the 13th-century fortified church to the cliff edge.

It has been voted the most beautiful village in France by public vote and the extraordinary views from the cliff edge were painted by Henri Martin for decades.

The village is freely walkable and those who visit in the early morning before the tourist coaches arrive from Cahors describe the combination of the extraordinary medieval architecture and the extraordinary position above the Lot valley as the most atmospherically beautiful perched village experience available anywhere in southwest France.
In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is a spectacular water staircase featuring eight oval-shaped basins, nine majestic gates, and a dramatic twenty-one-meter drop. #travel #France

Cascade du Bassin near Etretat on the Alabaster Coast of Normandy drops 26 metres from the chalk clifftop directly onto the beach below and is only visible and accessible for about two hours either side of low tide when the beach is exposed.

The extraordinary white chalk cliffs and natural arches of the Etretat coast surround it.

Those who time their visit to the Etretat coast to catch low tide and walk east from the main beach to find the cascade describe the experience of standing beneath a 26-metre waterfall on a white chalk beach with the Channel stretching ahead as one of the most surprising and most distinctive natural encounters available on any free beach in Normandy.
In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is a historic seventeenth-century waterway featuring a lush canopy of plane trees, unique oval locks, and remarkable masonry aqueducts. #travel #France

Lascaux near Montignac in the Dordogne was discovered in 1940 by four local teenagers and contains cave paintings estimated at 17,000 years old that represent the finest example of Palaeolithic art in the world.

The original cave is closed to protect the paintings and the extraordinary Lascaux IV international cave art centre opened in 2016 with a complete replica.

Entry to Lascaux IV costs around 20 euros.

Those who visit the extraordinary replica and see the Hall of the Bulls, the largest Palaeolithic painted chamber in the world, reproduced with complete fidelity describe the experience of walking through a 17,000-year-old painted cave as the most completely astonishing prehistoric art encounter available anywhere in France.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a historic marble capital featuring a majestic Romanesque cathedral, a sacred river source, and ancient streets lined with medieval stalls. #travel #France

The Camargue Natural Regional Park between the two arms of the Rhone delta in the Bouches-du-Rhone is home to the famous white Camargue horses, pink flamingos, black bulls, and an extraordinary concentration of waterbirds in a landscape of saltmarsh, reed bed, and lagoon that is unlike anywhere else in France.

The beaches of the Plage de Beauduc are some of the wildest in France.

Those who drive to the Beauduc beach in early spring when the flamingos are feeding in the lagoons beside the road and the Camargue horses are in the water meadows describe the combination of the extraordinary wildlife and the extraordinary wild beach as the most completely unexpected natural experience available within two hours of Marseille.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a picturesque medieval village featuring a 10th-century Carolingian tower, lush Mediterranean gardens, and panoramic views over the Orb River. #travel #France

Conques in the Aveyron is a perfectly preserved medieval pilgrimage village on the Camino de Santiago with the extraordinary Abbatiale Sainte-Foy, a masterpiece of Romanesque architecture whose extraordinary tympanum above the west portal is considered the finest piece of medieval religious sculpture in France.

The village of narrow lanes and medieval houses has barely changed since the 12th century. Entry to the abbey and its treasury is free.

Those who arrive in Conques in the early morning before the pilgrims and visitors arrive and sit in the extraordinary Romanesque abbey in near-silence describe it as the most atmospherically perfect and most architecturally extraordinary free religious heritage experience available anywhere in the French interior.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a dramatic river canyon featuring ancient troglodytic dwellings, crystal-clear swimming pools, and steep rocky cliffs. #travel #France

The Gorges de l'Ardeche in the southern Ardeche is a 30-kilometre canyon carved by the Ardeche river through the limestone plateau of the Bas-Vivarais, with walls rising up to 300 metres and the extraordinary Pont d'Arc natural arch spanning the river at the northern entrance.

The canyon can be kayaked in two days or walked along the rim path. The rim path above the gorge is freely accessible and the kayak descent costs around 30 euros for a full day rental.

Those who kayak the full length of the gorge in late May or September when the water level is right and the canyon walls are reflected in the river below describe it as the most dramatic and most completely immersive outdoor adventure available in southern France.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a dramatic river canyon featuring towering cliffs, cascading waterfalls, and crystal-clear natural pools. #travel #France

The Chauvet-Pont d'Arc cave in the Ardeche contains the oldest known cave paintings in the world, created by anatomically modern humans approximately 36,000 years ago.

The original cave is closed to protect the extraordinary paintings but the Caverne du Pont d'Arc replica opened in 2015 is an extraordinary full-scale reproduction using identical pigments and techniques.

Entry to the Caverne du Pont d'Arc costs around 15 euros.

Those who walk through the replica cave and stand before the extraordinary painted panel of lions, rhinoceroses, and horses describe the experience of engaging with art created 36,000 years ago as the most profoundly moving encounter with human prehistory available anywhere in France.
In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is a fortified medieval village featuring a 12th-century devil's bridge, a former castle keep, and winding cobblestone streets. #travel #France

Chateau de la Motte-Husson near Craon in the Mayenne is the finest example of an abandoned French chateau in the process of being restored by a young British couple who have been documenting the extraordinary decay and regeneration on social media since 2015.

The chateau's extraordinary Victorian interior, its painted ceilings, and its overgrown gardens have attracted millions of followers. The chateau is occasionally open for visitor days.

Those who follow the restoration process describe it as the most compelling and most emotionally engaging ongoing heritage restoration project available anywhere in France.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a medieval fortified village featuring dramatic limestone gorges, ancient cobblestone streets, and spectacular natural rock bridges. #travel #France

Cascade du Herisson near Clairvaux-les-Lacs in the Jura contains seven waterfalls along the Herisson river over a distance of 4 kilometres, of which the Eventail fall dropping 65 metres and the Grand Saut dropping 60 metres are the most spectacular.

The surrounding Jura landscape of lakes, meadows, and ancient forest is extraordinary. The full waterfall trail takes around 3 hours.

Those who walk the complete Herisson circuit in early June when the water is at its highest and the wildflower meadows of the Jura are at their most vivid describe it as the finest free waterfall hiking experience available anywhere in eastern France.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a majestic limestone mountain featuring dramatic cliffs, aromatic scrubland, and panoramic vineyard views. #travel #France

Roussillon in the Vaucluse is not blue but a village of extraordinary ochre, red, and yellow buildings built from and surrounded by 17 different shades of ochre soil that give it the most intense natural colour palette of any village in France.

The ochre quarry walk through the extraordinary coloured ravines is one of the finest free short walks in Provence.

The village is freely walkable and the ochre quarry circuit costs a small fee.

Those who walk through the ochre ravines in the late afternoon when the low light intensifies the extraordinary colour of the rock formations and the village above glows in the same palette describe it as the most visually extraordinary free natural and heritage experience available anywhere in the Luberon.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a vast dolomitic circus featuring towering limestone columns, a labyrinth of rock formations, and panoramic mountain views. #travel #France

The Pont du Gard near Nimes in the Gard is the tallest surviving Roman aqueduct in the world at 49 metres, built without mortar in the 1st century CE to carry water to the Roman city of Nemausus across the Gardon gorge. 

The three tiers of precisely fitted limestone arches are among the finest pieces of Roman engineering accessible to visitors anywhere in Europe.

Entry to the Pont du Gard site costs around 9 euros for pedestrians.

Those who swim in the Gardon river beneath the aqueduct in summer and look up at the extraordinary Roman structure rising above them describe it as the most dramatic and most humanly accessible ancient monument experience available in the south of France.
In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is a vibrant reservoir featuring iron-rich red earth, striking volcanic basalt, and sapphire blue waters. #travel #France

Les Baux-de-Provence in the Alpilles sits on a ridge of white limestone with the ruins of the medieval citadel rising above the village and the extraordinary Carrieres de Lumieres projection show housed in the ancient bauxite quarry below.

The village is one of the finest examples of a perched medieval Provencal settlement in France.

Entry to the village is free and the Carrieres de Lumieres costs around 15 euros.

Those who visit in the early morning before the day visitors arrive and walk through the medieval lanes to the extraordinary views from the citadel ruins over the Alpilles and the Camargue plain describe it as the finest perched village experience available in Provence.
In France, this hidden gem in Occitanie is a wild limestone canyon featuring high cliffs, clear swimming pools, and the oldest Roman bridge in the country. #travel #France

The Gorges de Holzarte near Larrau in the Basque Pyrenees requires a walk of around 2 hours to reach the extraordinary suspension bridge hung 180 metres above the gorge of the Olhadubi river.

The surrounding Iraty beech forest is the largest in western Europe and turns extraordinary gold in October.

The full circuit walk takes around 5 hours. Those who cross the suspension bridge in autumn when the beech forest above the gorge is at peak colour and the extraordinary view along the canyon is framed by golden trees describe it as the finest free adventure hiking experience available in the French Basque Pyrenees.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a medieval UNESCO village featuring a Romanesque abbey, narrow cobblestone streets, and a century-old plane tree. #travel #France

Plage de Saleccia on the Desert des Agriates coast of northern Corsica is accessible only by boat from Saint-Florent or by a 2-hour hike across the maquis and is consistently rated among the finest beaches in the Mediterranean.

The white sand, the crystal clear turquoise water, and the complete absence of any commercial development give it a quality of natural perfection that accessible beaches cannot match.

Boat taxis from Saint-Florent run in summer for around 25 euros return. Those who arrive on the first boat of the day and spend the morning on the beach before the day visitors arrive describe it as the most beautiful beach experience available anywhere in France.
In France, this hidden gem in Hérault is a horseshoe-shaped canyon featuring a 300-meter deep gorge, an ancient dried-up meander, and cascading waterfalls. #travel #France

Oradour-sur-Glane in the Haute-Vienne was destroyed by the SS Das Reich division on 10 June 1944, killing 643 of its residents.

The ruins of the village have been preserved exactly as they were left on that day, with the burned-out cars, the remains of the sewing machines in the dressmakers' shops, and the walls of the church where the women and children were burned.

Entry to the preserved village is free. Those who walk through the ruins and visit the memorial centre describe it as the most powerful and most emotionally devastating heritage experience available anywhere in France.

In France, this hidden gem in the Cévennes is a panoramic mountain summit featuring a historic weather observatory, diverse hiking trails, and sweeping views from the Alps to the Mediterranean. #travel #France

Cascade de Sillans near Sillans-la-Cascade in the Var drops 42 metres into a pool of extraordinary turquoise green, coloured by the limestone minerals in the water.

The surrounding forest of holm oak and strawberry trees gives the site a Mediterranean lushness completely unlike the arid garrigue landscape just a few kilometres away.

The walk from the village takes around 20 minutes on a well-marked path.

Those who visit on a weekday in May or October when the water is running well and the crowds are minimal describe it as the most beautiful and most unexpectedly tropical free waterfall experience available anywhere in Provence.

Cascade de Capèche is a lovely hidden waterfall in the forests of the Ariège, where clear water tumbles down mossy rocks into a shaded gorge.

Its peaceful setting makes it a refreshing stop on a woodland hike.

☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer when rainfall and snowmelt keep the falls flowing
🥾 Walk tip: The approach trail is short but can be muddy, sturdy shoes are recommended
📸 Don’t miss: Shots of the cascade framed by moss-covered boulders and green forest all around

Cascade de Burgeat is a small but charming waterfall tucked into the forests of the Ariège, where a stream flows over rock ledges into a calm pool.

The shaded woodland and gentle sound of water make it a peaceful spot for a short walk.

☀️ Best time: Spring when rainfall and snowmelt keep the stream lively
🥾 Walk tip: An easy forest path from the village of Burgeat leads directly to the falls
📸 Don’t miss: Close-up shots of the cascade with mossy rocks and overhanging branches creating a natural frame

Cascade de Boussès is a quiet waterfall hidden in the heart of the Pyrenees, where a clear mountain stream tumbles into a shaded, mossy gorge.

The sound of rushing water and the surrounding forest make it a tranquil stop on a woodland hike.

🥾 Walk tip: Trails are gentle but can be damp, wear sturdy shoes for the forest paths

Cascade de Blangy is a pretty waterfall near Hirson in northern France, where the Gland River tumbles over rocky ledges into a wooded gorge.

🥾 Walk tip: Follow the short riverside paths for different viewpoints of the falls
📸 Don’t miss: Shots of the cascade framed by mossy rocks and leafy trees

Cascade de Bibet is a charming little waterfall tucked into the green hills of the Pyrenees, where a clear stream flows over mossy rock ledges into a shaded pool.

Its quiet forest setting makes it a refreshing stop on a local walk.

📍 Where: Cascade de Bibet, near Bagnères-de-Bigorre, Hautes-Pyrénées, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer when rainfall and snowmelt feed the flow
🥾 Walk tip: A short woodland path leads to the falls, suitable for a relaxed stroll
📸 Don’t miss: Close-up shots of the water spilling gently over moss-covered rocks

Cascade de Bérard is a picturesque waterfall near Vallorcine in the Chamonix Valley, tumbling through a rocky gorge surrounded by dense alpine forest.

A wooden bridge and viewpoint let you get close to the spray while enjoying the dramatic mountain backdrop.

📍 Where: Cascade de Bérard, Vallorcine, Haute-Savoie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring through summer when snowmelt makes the falls thunderous
🥾 Walk tip: A short, easy walk from Le Buet village leads to the falls, family-friendly and scenic
📸 Don’t miss: Photos from the footbridge spanning the gorge with the cascade rushing below

Cascade Darre Splumouse is a wild mountain waterfall in the Pyrenees, thundering down a rocky gorge surrounded by steep alpine slopes.

Its remote setting makes it a rewarding sight for hikers exploring the higher valleys.

📍 Where: Cascade Darre Splumouse, Gavarnie area, Hautes-Pyrénées, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring and summer when snowmelt keeps the falls at full strength
🥾 Walk tip: The approach is a demanding alpine hike, best suited for experienced walkers with proper gear
📸 Don’t miss: Wide shots capturing the cascade against rugged peaks and high mountain scenery

Cascade d’Escane Gat is a tucked-away waterfall in the Pyrénées-Orientales, where a narrow stream drops into a shaded gorge surrounded by rugged rock and forest.

Its name, meaning “broken cat,” adds a touch of local legend to the wild setting.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Escane Gat, near Vernet-les-Bains, Pyrénées-Orientales, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring when snowmelt and rain feed the falls
🥾 Walk tip: The trail is short but can be rocky, wear sturdy shoes for the descent
📸 Don’t miss: Photos from the base looking up at the cascade framed by steep cliffs and greenery

Cascade d’Enfer, or “Hell’s Waterfall,” is a dramatic Pyrenean cascade that plunges through a rocky gorge surrounded by dense forest.

Despite its fierce name, the setting is tranquil, with cool mist rising from the falls on hot summer days.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Enfer, near Bagnères-de-Luchon, Haute-Garonne, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer when snowmelt swells the falls
🥾 Walk tip: The trail is moderately steep but rewarding, leading through shaded woodland
📸 Don’t miss: Long-exposure shots capturing the waterfall’s powerful white stream against dark rock walls

Cascade d’en Bas is a beautiful waterfall in Les Contamines-Montjoie, tumbling into a rocky gorge surrounded by alpine forest.

It’s the lower of two falls in the valley, and the path down offers peaceful views of rushing water and mountain greenery.

📍 Where: Cascade d’en Bas, Les Contamines-Montjoie, Haute-Savoie, France
☀️ Best time: Summer when trails are clear and the flow is steady with glacier melt

Cascade d’Eilloux is a serene waterfall tucked into the forests of the Ariège, where a slender stream flows over mossy rock ledges into a shaded pool.

Its quiet setting makes it a refreshing stop on a woodland walk.

☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer when rainfall and snowmelt feed the cascade
🥾 Walk tip: Follow the marked forest path from the village, it’s short but can be muddy after rain

Cascade d’Ars is one of the most impressive waterfalls in the French Pyrenees, plunging in three dramatic tiers with a total drop of nearly 250 meters.

In spring, the snowmelt makes it roar with power, filling the valley with mist.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Ars, near Aulus-les-Bains, Ariège, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring to early summer when the flow is at its peak
🥾 Walk tip: The hike from Aulus-les-Bains is about 3 hours round trip with steady elevation gain, bring water and good shoes
📸 Don’t miss: Wide shots from the trail where you can capture all three tiers cascading down the cliff

Cascade d’Arizes is a graceful waterfall tucked into the Pyrenees, where clear mountain water spills over a rock face into a quiet forest hollow.

The setting feels peaceful and remote, with only the sound of rushing water breaking the silence.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Arizes, Hautes-Pyrénées, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer when snowmelt makes the falls strongest
🥾 Walk tip: A moderate hike through forest paths leads to the base, sturdy shoes recommended
📸 Don’t miss: A vertical shot capturing the slender cascade framed by mossy cliffs and green trees

Cascade d’Arcouzan is a wild, untamed waterfall high in the Pyrenees, fed by the melting glaciers of Mont Valier.

The powerful stream tumbles through a steep gorge, surrounded by dense forest and dramatic mountain scenery.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Arcouzan, near Seix, Ariège, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring and summer when snowmelt swells the flow
🥾 Walk tip: The approach is long and steep, best for experienced hikers with good gear
📸 Don’t miss: Shots of the cascade with Mont Valier towering in the background

Cascade d’Anterne is a spectacular high-mountain waterfall that crashes down a cliff on the way to Lac d’Anterne in the Mont Blanc Massif.

Surrounded by alpine meadows and jagged peaks, it’s a wild and dramatic stop on a longer hike.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Anterne, Sixt-Fer-à-Cheval, Haute-Savoie, France

🥾 Walk tip: Reach it via the challenging hike toward Lac d’Anterne, expect a long day with steep sections

Cascade d’Angon is a dramatic waterfall near Lake Annecy, plunging nearly 60 meters into a narrow gorge surrounded by steep cliffs.

☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer when the snowmelt makes the falls thunderous

Cascade d’Allières is a charming waterfall hidden in the Jura Mountains, where a clear stream tumbles over limestone rock into a shaded forest pool.

Cascade d’Aiguebelle is a tall, graceful waterfall tucked into the Maurienne Valley, where water streams down a cliffside surrounded by forest.

Cascade d’Aiglière is a picturesque waterfall in the French Alps, where a mountain stream tumbles down a rocky cliff into a cool basin surrounded by pines.

Cascade du Ray is a graceful waterfall tucked into the limestone cliffs of the Jura, where water streams down in thin veils into a calm, shaded pool.

Cascade du Puits Neuf is a lovely hidden fall in the Jura, where a narrow stream plunges into a shaded gorge surrounded by moss and ferns.

Cascade du Pontet is a peaceful waterfall tucked in the Hautes-Alpes, where a mountain stream tumbles down through rocks and pine forest.

Cascade du Pont du Diable is a dramatic waterfall in the Haute-Savoie, where water rushes through a narrow gorge beneath the “Devil’s Bridge.”

Cascade du Moulin de la Fraise is a small but picturesque waterfall hidden in the forests of the Ardennes.

Cascade du Moulin d’Aval is a charming waterfall nestled in the Breton countryside, where a small stream tumbles past an old mill and into a shaded pool.

Surrounded by mossy stones and leafy woodland, it has a quiet, storybook feel.

Cascade du Moine in France is a tucked-away waterfall in the Jura Mountains, where cool forest air, mossy rocks, and the sound of rushing water create a peaceful escape. 

It’s a great spot to pause during a hike and enjoy the quiet beauty of the region.

📍 Where: Cascade du Moine, Jura Mountains, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France

One of France’s most famous waterfall trails, the Cascades du Hérisson tumble through a series of seven major falls and countless smaller ones in the Jura.

From the thundering 60-meter Cascade de l’Éventail to the delicate lace of smaller drops, the valley feels like a living water garden.

📍 Where: Cascades du Hérisson, Jura Mountains, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France
☀️ Best time: Spring, when snowmelt and rain create the strongest flow, or autumn when the surrounding forest blazes with color
🥾 Walk tip: The full trail is about 7 km return, wear sturdy shoes and take your time, as the path can be slippery near the cascades.
📸 Don’t miss: A long-exposure shot of Cascade de l’Éventail, where the wide curtain of water fans over limestone ledges.

Tucked into the pine forests of the Cantal, Cascade du Gué du Saut plunges dramatically into a rocky gorge carved by the Maronne River.

Its wild setting and echoing roar make it one of the most striking waterfalls in Auvergne’s volcanic highlands.

📸 Don’t miss: A wide shot from the overlook above, where the cascade drops into a dark gorge framed by dense green forest.

Tucked into the green valleys of the Jura, Cascade du Grand Val falls in a fine white stream over limestone cliffs into a shaded pool.

Surrounded by forest and echoing with birdsong, it’s a peaceful retreat just steps from quiet village paths.

📍 Where: Cascade du Grand Val, Jura Mountains, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when rainfall and snowmelt feed the falls and the forest glows fresh and green
🥾 Walk tip: The approach is gentle and family-friendly, but the rocks at the base are slippery, good shoes are essential.
📸 Don’t miss: A photo from the footbridge below, where the cascade drops cleanly against mossy limestone walls.

Hidden in the forests of Haute-Savoie, Cascade du Grand Ruisseau tumbles over mossy rock ledges in a cool, shaded glen. Its gentle tiers and tranquil pool make it a refreshing stop on mountain walks near Sixt-Fer-à-Cheval.

📍 Where: Cascade du Grand Ruisseau, Haute-Savoie, Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when snowmelt feeds the stream and the forest is alive with fresh greenery
🥾 Walk tip: The approach is short but can be slippery after rain, wear sturdy shoes and allow extra time to enjoy the shaded forest paths.
📸 Don’t miss: A close-up shot of the cascade flowing over moss-covered stone, framed by ferns and dappled light.

Dropping into a rocky gorge near Volvic, Cascade du Gour Saillant is a striking waterfall framed by dark volcanic stone and lush greenery.

The water fans out in a white curtain before tumbling into a clear pool at the base.

📍 Where: Cascade du Gour Saillant, Volvic, Puy-de-Dôme, Auvergne, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when rainfall and snowmelt feed the falls and the forest is at its brightest green
🥾 Walk tip: The walk from the village is short but the rocks around the falls are slick, wear good shoes if you plan to get close.
📸 Don’t miss: A long-exposure shot from the pool below, where the cascade spills like a veil over the volcanic cliff.

Nestled in a dramatic gorge near Saint-Étienne, Cascade du Gouffre d’Enfer pours from a high dam into a rocky chasm framed by steep forested slopes.

The thundering water and wild setting give the place a name worthy of legend “the Abyss of Hell.”

📍 Where: Cascade du Gouffre d’Enfer, near Saint-Étienne, Loire, Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, France
☀️ Best time: Spring, when rains and snowmelt swell the reservoir and the cascade roars into the gorge
🥾 Walk tip: Follow the marked trails from the Gouffre d’Enfer dam, expect some steep climbs but rewarding views of the valley.
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the waterfall framed by the dam and the sheer rock walls of the gorge, especially dramatic after heavy rain.

Tumbling from the cliffs of the Fiz massif, Cascade du Dérochoir is a spectacular alpine fall that plunges into the valley near Passy.

Surrounded by jagged peaks and meadows, it’s a wild and powerful sight that marks the gateway to the mountains.

📍 Where: Cascade du Dérochoir, Passy, Haute-Savoie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring and early summer, when snowmelt feeds the falls and the surrounding alpine pastures bloom
🥾 Walk tip: The approach from Plaine Joux is steep and rocky, wear sturdy boots and allow plenty of time for the mountain trail.
📸 Don’t miss: A wide shot from the valley floor, capturing the falls plunging down the cliffs with Mont Blanc in the distance.

Dropping nearly 30 meters from a basalt cliff, Cascade du Déroc reveals a dramatic curtain of water framed by wildflowers and the open plateaus of the Aubrac. Behind the falls, a rocky cave carved by lava offers a hidden vantage point on this striking cascade.

📍 Where: Cascade du Déroc, Nasbinals, Lozère, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when snowmelt and rain feed the waterfall and the meadows are alive with color
🥾 Walk tip: The short trail from Nasbinals is easy, but wear sturdy shoes if you plan to explore the slippery rocks near the base.
📸 Don’t miss: A photo from inside the lava cave, looking out through the falling water toward the Aubrac landscape.

Crashing down a rocky cliff near Chamonix, Cascade du Dard pours into a forested ravine with Mont Blanc towering just beyond.

The thundering water and alpine backdrop make it one of the valley’s most dramatic natural sights.

📍 Where: Cascade du Dard, Chamonix-Mont-Blanc, Haute-Savoie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring and summer, when snowmelt swells the Arveyron River and the falls roar at full strength
🥾 Walk tip: A short but steep path leads from the road to the falls, wear sturdy shoes and be cautious on wet, rocky ground.
📸 Don’t miss: A shot framing the cascade with Mont Blanc rising in the distance, especially striking on a clear day.

Tucked into a wooded gorge, Cascade du Creux de l’Oulette spills over dark rock into a clear basin surrounded by moss and ferns.

Quiet and shaded, it feels like a secret corner of the Auvergne where water and forest blend in harmony.

📍 Where: Cascade du Creux de l’Oulette, Saint-Anthème, Puy-de-Dôme, Auvergne, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when rainfall and snowmelt give the falls their fullest flow
🥾 Walk tip: The approach is short but can be muddy, wear sturdy shoes and take care if scrambling on the rocks near the pool.
📸 Don’t miss: A photo from the base, where the water tumbles elegantly through the greenery of the narrow gorge.

Hidden in the forests of the Jura, Cascade du Creux slips over mossy limestone ledges into a shaded gorge.

Its delicate streams and tranquil pool make it a quiet retreat surrounded by ferns, beech trees, and the sound of rushing water.

📍 Where: Cascade du Creux, Jura Mountains, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France
☀️ Best time: Spring, when rainfall swells the flow and the forest bursts into fresh greenery
🥾 Walk tip: The path is short but uneven, wear sturdy shoes, especially after rain when the rocks are slick.
📸 Don’t miss: A long-exposure shot from the base, where the water’s silky threads tumble over moss-covered stone.

At the heart of a vast limestone canyon, the Cascade du Cirque de Navacelles tumbles into a turquoise pool surrounded by cliffs and terraces carved by the Vis River.

The setting is dramatic yet peaceful, a natural amphitheater where water has shaped the land for millennia.

📍 Where: Cascade du Cirque de Navacelles, Hérault/Gard, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when the Vis River runs strong and the surrounding meadows are green

Tucked deep in the Ariège Pyrenees, Cascade de l’Artigue is a wild mountain waterfall pouring through a rocky gorge surrounded by pine forest.

Remote and untamed, it’s a reward for hikers venturing into one of the quietest corners of the Pyrenees.

📍 Where: Cascade de l’Artigue, Ariège, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Late spring and early summer, when snowmelt feeds the strongest flow
🥾 Walk tip: The hike in is moderately challenging and steep in sections, bring water, good shoes, and be ready for alpine weather changes
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the powerful cascade framed by rugged cliffs and green forest, with mist rising into the valley

Tucked in the Jura mountains, Cascade de l’Aigle is a graceful waterfall hidden among forested slopes and limestone cliffs.

Its name, “Eagle Falls,” reflects the wild, untamed feel of the valley where it flows.

📍 Where: Cascade de l’Aigle, Jura, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when snowmelt and rain swell the falls
🥾 Walk tip: Trails can be muddy after rain, wear sturdy shoes and take your time on the forest paths
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the falls framed by mossy rocks and green forest, with soft mist rising from the pool

Set against the rugged cliffs of Réunion Island’s south coast, Cascade de Grand Fond is a dramatic waterfall plunging straight into the sea.

Remote and wild, it’s one of the island’s most striking natural scenes, where mountain streams meet the vast Indian Ocean.

📍 Where: Cascade de Grand Fond, Réunion Island, France (Indian Ocean)
☀️ Best time: After heavy rains, when the waterfall surges down the cliffs in full force
🥾 Walk tip: The falls are best admired from coastal viewpoints, the terrain is steep and wild, so stick to marked paths
📸 Don’t miss: A wide shot of the cascade spilling from the cliffs into the crashing waves below

Plunging 60 meters into a lush basin, Cascade de Glandieu is one of the most striking waterfalls in the Ain region.

Surrounded by greenery and easy to reach from the village, it’s a perfect stop for a refreshing pause in the French countryside.

📍 Where: Cascade de Glandieu, Ain, Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early summer, when rainfall feeds the strongest flow
🥾 Walk tip: The falls are right in the village with minimal walking, bring a picnic and enjoy the shady spots nearby
📸 Don’t miss: A full-height shot of the white water plunging down the mossy cliff into the turquoise pool below

Built in the 19th century as part of Nice’s water supply system, Cascade de Gairaut is both a man-made waterfall and a romantic garden escape.

With its rustic chalet, stone bridges, and sweeping views over the city, it’s a peaceful retreat just above the Riviera.

📍 Where: Cascade de Gairaut, Nice, Alpes-Maritimes, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and autumn for mild weather and clear city views from the hillside
🥾 Walk tip: The park is small and easy to explore, take time to enjoy the details of the chalet and surrounding gardens
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the waterfall framed by the wooden chalet, with Nice and the Mediterranean glimmering in the distance

Tumbling down the cliffs above the village, Cascade de Creissels creates a dramatic backdrop to this corner of southern France.

Fed by underground springs, the falls appear strongest after heavy rain, adding to the charm of the medieval streets below.

📍 Where: Cascade de Creissels, Aveyron, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and after rainfall, when the waterfalls flow at their fullest
🥾 Walk tip: Stroll through the village of Creissels, the best views of the falls are found from its narrow lanes and stone bridges
📸 Don’t miss: A wide shot of the cascade pouring down the cliffs with the tiled rooftops of Creissels in the foreground

Falling 40 meters into a limestone basin, Cascade de Courmes is a spectacular waterfall tucked in the hills of Provence.

Surrounded by cliffs and greenery, it’s a refreshing stop for hikers exploring the Alpes-Maritimes.

📍 Where: Cascade de Courmes, near Gourdon, Alpes-Maritimes, France
☀️ Best time: Spring or after heavy rain, when the flow is strongest
🥾 Walk tip: The short walk from the village is easy, but trails around the gorge give you different perspectives of the falls
📸 Don’t miss: A shot from below, capturing the full height of the cascade framed by sheer rock walls

One of the most impressive waterfalls in the Bugey region, Cascade de Cerveyrieu plunges more than 60 metres into a limestone gorge.

Surrounded by cliffs and forest, it’s a wild and dramatic sight that showcases the power of the Ain’s waterways.

📍 Where: Cascade de Cerveyrieu, near Artemare, Ain, Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, France
🌿 Best time: Spring and early summer when rainfall and snowmelt make the falls thunder at full force
🥾 Walk tip: The trail to the viewing point is short but steep, bring sturdy shoes and take care on wet ground
📸 Don’t miss: A wide shot from across the gorge, capturing the waterfall’s full height against the rocky cliffs

Hidden in the forests of the Morvan, Cascade de Brisecou is a 35-metre waterfall tumbling over mossy rock into a shady pool.

Peaceful and less crowded than other falls in the region, it’s a perfect spot to pause and soak in Burgundy’s wilder side.

📍 Where: Cascade de Brisecou, near Avallon, Yonne, Burgundy, France
🌿 Best time: Spring and after rainfall, when the cascade flows strongest through the gorge
🥾 Walk tip: The short forest trail to the falls can be muddy, wear good shoes, especially after rain
📸 Don’t miss: A long-exposure shot from the base, with the white curtain of water framed by deep green woodland

Where the Bonheur River disappears into a cave and reemerges in a dramatic torrent, the Cascade de Bramabiau is one of the Cévennes’ most spectacular natural sights. 

Known as the “birthplace of French speleology,” it’s a place where underground rivers meet the open gorge.

📍 Where: Cascade de Bramabiau, near Camprieu, Gard, Occitanie, France
🌿 Best time: Spring when snowmelt feeds the underground river and the cascade is at its strongest
🥾 Walk tip: The visit is part of a guided tour through the cave system, bring a jacket, as it stays cool inside
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the river bursting out of the cave mouth into the sunlight, framed by mossy cliffs

Dropping 40 metres in a single leap, Cascade de Baume is one of Auvergne’s most striking waterfalls.

Surrounded by volcanic cliffs and dense forest, its dramatic plunge makes it a favorite stop for nature lovers near Le Puy-en-Velay.

📍 Where: Cascade de Baume, Solignac-sur-Loire, Haute-Loire, Auvergne, France
🌿 Best time: Spring and early summer when the falls run strongest from snowmelt and rain
🥾 Walk tip: The trail to the base is short but steep, sturdy shoes are a must
📸 Don’t miss: A shot from below capturing the full height of the cascade framed by mossy rock walls

A hidden gem in Corsica’s rugged interior, the Cascade de Bâle tumbles through granite rock into crystal-clear pools surrounded by maquis scrub and forest.

It’s a tranquil spot where you can cool off in fresh mountain water while enjoying wild island scenery.

📍 Where: Cascade de Bâle, near Venaco, Haute-Corse, Corsica, France
🌿 Best time: Late spring and early summer when the flow is strong from snowmelt and rains
🥾 Walk tip: The path to the falls is short but rocky, wear good shoes for scrambling
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the cascade spilling into the turquoise pool, framed by Corsica’s green hills

Falling 30 metres into a green limestone cirque, Cascade d’Autoire is one of the most beautiful waterfalls in the Lot.

Surrounded by cliffs and woodland, it’s a refreshing stop on a hike that also passes by medieval ruins and dramatic viewpoints.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Autoire, near Autoire, Lot, Occitanie, France
🌿 Best time: Spring and early summer when rainfall feeds the cascade and the valley is lush
🥾 Walk tip: The loop trail combines the waterfall with the Château des Anglais ruins high on the cliffs ,wear sturdy shoes
📸 Don’t miss: A framed shot of the waterfall from beneath the overhanging limestone ledges

Hidden in the cliffs above Lake Annecy, Cascade d’Angon is a spectacular 60-metre waterfall that tumbles into a narrow gorge.

The trail to reach it winds through forest and rocky passages, rewarding hikers with one of Haute-Savoie’s most dramatic sights.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Angon, Talloires, Lake Annecy, Haute-Savoie, France
🌿 Best time: Late spring and early summer when the waterfall is at its strongest from snowmelt
🥾 Walk tip: The path includes ledges and chains along the rock, not difficult, but take care with footing, especially when wet
📸 Don’t miss: A shot from the base of the gorge looking up at the falls streaming down the cliff face

Tucked into the forests of the Mont-Dore area, Cascade d’Allières is a graceful waterfall surrounded by lush greenery and volcanic rock.

The cool mist and shady setting make it a refreshing stop on a hike through Auvergne’s dramatic landscapes.

📍 Where: Cascade d’Allières, Mont-Dore, Puy-de-Dôme, Auvergne, France
🌿 Best time: Spring and early summer when snowmelt feeds the falls and the forest is vibrant
🥾 Walk tip: The trail is moderate with some rocky sections, sturdy shoes recommended
📸 Don’t miss: A long-exposure shot to capture the silky flow of the cascade framed by mossy trees

A storybook citadel rising above the Aude River, Carcassonne is one of Europe’s best-preserved medieval fortresses.

Its double walls, 52 towers, and cobbled lanes feel straight out of history, while the surrounding vineyards and countryside add to the magic.

📍 Where: Carcassonne, Occitanie, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and autumn for pleasant weather and fewer crowds inside the walled city
🥾 Walk tip: Stroll the ramparts for sweeping views of the Pyrenees and the rolling hills of Languedoc
📸 Don’t miss: A twilight shot of the illuminated walls glowing golden against the darkening sky

Rising high above the Emerald Coast, Cap Fréhel is a dramatic headland of pink sandstone cliffs and windswept heathland.

With panoramic views stretching from Jersey to Mont Saint-Michel on a clear day, it’s one of Brittany’s most breathtaking coastal spots.

📍 Where: Cap Fréhel, Côtes-d’Armor, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and summer when the heath blooms with wildflowers and seabirds nest along the cliffs
🥾 Walk tip: Follow the GR34 coastal path from Fort La Latte to Cap Fréhel for one of the region’s most scenic walks
📸 Don’t miss: A wide shot of the cliffs at golden hour with the Cap Fréhel lighthouse standing guard above the sea

A rugged peninsula jutting into the Mediterranean, Cap Corse is Corsica at its wildest.

With winding coastal roads, perched villages, and hidden coves, every turn reveals dramatic views of sea and mountain.

Known as the “island within the island,” it’s a paradise for road-trippers and hikers alike.

📍 Where: Cap Corse, Haute-Corse, Corsica, France
☀️ Best time: Spring and early autumn for mild weather, blooming maquis, and fewer crowds
🥾 Walk tip: Try a section of the Sentier des Douaniers coastal path for stunning cliffs and turquoise waters
📸 Don’t miss: A shot from the Mattei Windmill viewpoint, capturing the peninsula stretching into the sea

Running along the seaside at La Baule, the Boulevard de l’Atlantique is a promenade of sweeping ocean views, elegant villas, and the lively energy of France’s most famous bay.

Perfect for strolling, cycling, or simply watching the waves roll in, it captures the charm of the Atlantic coast in style.

📍 Where: Boulevard de l’Atlantique, La Baule-Escoublac, Loire-Atlantique, France
☀️ Best time: Summer evenings for a walk when the promenade comes alive, or autumn for quieter seaside views
🥾 Walk tip: Rent a bike to follow the boulevard’s full length, linking beaches, cafés, and scenic outlooks
📸 Don’t miss: Sunset over La Baule Bay with the curve of the promenade lit up along the shoreline

Perched above the Côte d’Azur, Bormes-les-Mimosas is a medieval village draped in flowers and bathed in sunshine.

Its pastel lanes, stone archways, and bougainvillea-draped houses give way to views of the Mediterranean, with golden beaches just below.

📍 Where: Bormes-les-Mimosas, Var, Provence-Alpes-Côte d’Azur, France
🌸 Best time: February for the famous Mimosa Festival, or summer for combining village walks with nearby beaches
🥾 Walk tip: Wander up through the old village to the castle ruins for sweeping views over the coast and Îles d’Hyères
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the flower-filled streets glowing in late afternoon light against the blue sea backdrop

A sheltered cove with golden sand and calm waters, Plage de Boisvinet is a favorite for families on the Vendée coast.

Overlooked by a pretty lighthouse and backed by a promenade, it offers safe swimming, sailing, and a classic seaside holiday feel.

📍 Where: Plage de Boisvinet, Saint-Gilles-Croix-de-Vie, Vendée, Pays de la Loire, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming and sailing lessons, or spring for relaxed walks along the jetty
🥾 Walk tip: Stroll out to the lighthouse for sweeping views across the bay and town
📸 Don’t miss: A sunset shot from the jetty with the beach, lighthouse, and fishing boats in the frame

With golden sand and calm waters, Plage de Bénodet is the heart of this seaside resort at the mouth of the Odet River.

Backed by a promenade, cafés, and sailing clubs, it blends classic Breton charm with a lively holiday atmosphere.

📍 Where: Plage de Bénodet, Finistère, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming, sailing, and lively beach days, or spring for quieter walks along the promenade
🥾 Walk tip: Stroll the Corniche de l’Estuaire path for views of the Odet River meeting the sea
📸 Don’t miss: A sunrise shot across the bay when the first light sparkles on the water

Long and sweeping, Plage de Pen Guen stretches out beneath Saint-Cast-le-Guildo with fine sand and open views across Saint-Malo Bay.

At low tide the beach seems endless, perfect for walking, beachcombing, or simply soaking up the fresh Breton air.

📍 Where: Plage de Pen Guen, Saint-Cast-le-Guildo, Côtes-d’Armor, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming and family days out, or spring and autumn for quiet coastal walks
🥾 Walk tip: Head toward the western end at low tide to spot mussel beds and tidal pools
📸 Don’t miss: A wide-angle shot at sunset when the receding tide mirrors the colors of the sky

Nestled between rocky cliffs and tidal pools, Plage du Grouin is a quiet beach on Brittany’s Emerald Coast.

With views out toward the Îles Chausey and Mont Saint-Michel Bay, it’s a place where rugged scenery meets peaceful shoreline.

📍 Where: Plage du Grouin, near Cancale, Ille-et-Vilaine, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Low tide for exploring rock pools and coastal walks, or late afternoon for golden light on the cliffs
🥾 Walk tip: Combine a beach visit with the coastal path around Pointe du Grouin for dramatic sea views
📸 Don’t miss: A shot from the headland capturing the beach below framed by rocky cliffs and open sea

Sheltered by islands and rocky headlands, Plage de Port-Blanc is a picturesque Breton beach with calm, clear waters and soft sand.

Popular with families and sailors, it’s also a gateway to coastal walks that reveal some of Brittany’s most dramatic seascapes.

📍 Where: Plage de Port-Blanc, Penvenan, Côtes-d’Armor, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming and sailing, or early autumn for quieter seaside strolls
🥾 Walk tip: Follow the Sentier des Douaniers (GR34) for sweeping coastal views dotted with islets
📸 Don’t miss: A panoramic shot at low tide when rocky outcrops and small islands emerge from the sea

Set at the edge of pine forest and dunes, Plage de La Palmyre offers a mix of calm stretches for families and wilder Atlantic surf for adventurers.

With views of the Bonne Anse Bay and easy access to cycling trails, it’s a classic Charente-Maritime getaway.

📍 Where: Plage de La Palmyre, Les Mathes, Charente-Maritime, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming and water sports, or spring for quieter walks among the dunes
🥾 Walk tip: Bring a bike, the coastal cycle paths through the forest are some of the best in the region
📸 Don’t miss: A sunset shot from the dunes with the lighthouse of La Coubre in the distance

A broad sweep of golden sand framed by grassy dunes, Plage de Mané Guen is a relaxed and family-friendly beach on Brittany’s south coast.

With calm waters for swimming and plenty of space for kite-flying or beach games, it’s a favorite with locals in summer.

📍 Where: Plage de Mané Guen, Plouharnel, Morbihan, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for safe swimming and lively beach days, or autumn for peaceful coastal walks
🥾 Walk tip: The beach connects to nearby stretches of sand, making it ideal for long seaside strolls
📸 Don’t miss: Sunset over the Atlantic when the sand glows and the dunes cast long shadows

A vast and wild stretch of sand facing the Atlantic, Plage du Magouëro is known for its natural beauty and sweeping views.

Backed by dunes and pine forest, it’s a quieter alternative to nearby resorts, perfect for long walks and dramatic sunsets.

📍 Where: Plage du Magouëro, Plouhinec, Morbihan, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer evenings for golden light and cooling sea breezes
🥾 Walk tip: Access the beach via sandy paths through the dunes, bring good shoes and be mindful of protected areas
📸 Don’t miss: A wide-angle sunset shot with the endless horizon and dunes glowing behind you

Framed by a historic fort and a sheltered bay, Plage de Kernevest is a peaceful beach with soft sand and calm waters.

Popular for swimming, paddleboarding, and family picnics, it also offers a touch of history with Fort de Kernevest watching over the shoreline.

📍 Where: Plage de Kernevest, Saint-Philibert, Morbihan, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for safe swimming and water sports, or spring for quiet walks by the fort
🥾 Walk tip: Explore the coastal path around the bay for views of islands and sailboats
📸 Don’t miss: A shot of the beach with the fort in the background, glowing in the late afternoon sun

Sheltered within a calm bay, Plage de Kerléven is a family-friendly beach with soft sand, gentle waves, and a laid-back Breton charm.

Sailing boats often dot the horizon, and the wide shallows make it ideal for paddling or long barefoot walks.

📍 Where: Plage de Kerléven, La Forêt-Fouesnant, Finistère, Brittany, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming and sailing, or early autumn for quieter coastal strolls
🥾 Walk tip: Wander the promenade behind the beach for views of the bay and the nearby marina
📸 Don’t miss: A shot at low tide when the boats rest on the sand, framed by the curve of the bay

A long ribbon of soft sand backed by dunes and pines, Plage des Gollandières is one of the most popular beaches on Île de Ré.

Calm waters and plenty of space make it perfect for swimming, family days out, and sunset strolls along the Atlantic.

📍 Where: Plage des Gollandières, Le Bois-Plage-en-Ré, Île de Ré, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France
☀️ Best time: Summer for swimming and lively beach days, or September for quieter walks when the crowds thin
🥾 Walk tip: Rent a bike and follow the island’s cycle paths, this beach is easily reached on two wheels
📸 Don’t miss: Sunset over the dunes when the sky glows pink and orange above the wide Atlantic horizon
